review_summary: The transcript gives enough grounded detail for two questions on CLIP’s practical strengths and limitations.

question_bank.question: Why is CLIP especially useful for retrieval and open-vocabulary use?
choices: Because images and text share an embedding space where similarity can be computed efficiently with dot products
Because it must retrain its text encoder for every query
Because it stores a segmentation mask for every training image
Because it converts every image into a caption before comparing examples
question_bank.explanation: Once images and text are embedded into the same space, fast similarity search becomes possible, and users can query with arbitrary text rather than a fixed closed set of labels.

question_bank.question: Which example best reflects a known CLIP limitation discussed here?
choices: Confusing “a mug in grass” with “grass in a mug” because relationships are not grounded reliably
Failing only when the image resolution is below 224x224
Being unable to compare any two text queries with each other
Requiring a segmentation mask for every caption during pretraining
question_bank.explanation: Contrastive image-level supervision can capture the presence of concepts without fully representing how those concepts relate to each other or where they are in the scene.
